query_text:
Long-context LLMs accumulate large KV caches that dominate
memory bandwidth; how can we compress or selectively evict
cache entries while preserving the model's ability to attend
to distant relevant tokens?

round_records.method:
We propose Predictive Hierarchical KV (PHKV), a three-part caching system that keeps exact recent context, compresses the distant past into a small set of multi-aspect summary slots per chunk, and recalls exact keys/values on demand via a lightweight predictor and an ANN index. At runtime, the model holds a full-fidelity sliding window (e.g., last W tokens) in DRAM. Older tokens are grouped into fixed-size chunks; each chunk is replaced by k summary KV slots that approximate the chunk’s attention behavior. A tiny predictor, queried with the current query vector Q_t and cheap metadata, decides which summaries (and, when needed, which exact evicted tokens) to materialize for attention at this step. Evicted exact KV entries are quantized and parked off-DRAM together with an ANN index over their keys to enable fast recall when Q_t demands it.

Chunk compression is multi-view and hierarchical. For each aging chunk, we cluster or low-rank factorize its key matrix to produce k centroids that act like viewer tokens capturing different semantic aspects of the chunk; corresponding summary values are fitted (e.g., via attention-weighted regression) so that attending to these k slots approximates attending to the full chunk. As chunks age further, we pyramid-merge summaries across adjacent chunks (k -> k/2) to cap memory growth while preserving a coarse global signal. Summaries retain position encodings (e.g., via centroided rotary phases) so relative alignment is not lost, and all summaries and their small per-slot stats (token counts, last-hit time) remain resident; full KVs are evicted.

Eviction and recall are predictor-guided to save bandwidth. A small MLP or logistic head takes features such as dot-products between Q_t and each summary key, recency, usage, and entropy of past attentions to predict which summaries will receive nontrivial attention mass. Only predicted-relevant summaries are exposed to the attention kernel. If a chosen summary’s pre-softmax score crosses a threshold, we probe the off-DRAM ANN index with Q_t (or the summary key) to fetch the top-r exact KVs from the corresponding chunk and transiently splice them into the attention computation, replacing or augmenting the coarse slot. This prefetch is quantized and batched across layers to amortize I/O. A strict bandwidth budget governs how many exact KVs can be pulled per step; when exceeded, we fall back to the summaries (graceful degradation).

Training makes the model robust to partial and summarized caches and teaches the predictor what to keep. We fine-tune with KV-dropout that randomly replaces old chunks by their k-slot summaries (and zero-pads missing entries) to match target retention rates, so the decoder learns to operate when only summaries are present. In parallel, we generate pseudo-relevance labels for chunks: for a small subset of steps during fine-tuning, we measure the change in next-token loss when including vs excluding a chunk (or its exact top-r tokens) and train the predictor to classify chunks as helpful or not; simple cosine-similarity gates with Q_t serve as a cheap proxy feature during inference. Distillation from full-cache teacher runs can further shape the summary fitting and predictor thresholds. At deployment, PHKV delivers: exact KVs for the recent window; k-per-chunk summaries for the distant past; a predictor that minimizes unnecessary memory traffic; and an ANN-backed, on-the-fly recall path that restores fidelity only where the model would otherwise under-attend to distant but relevant tokens.
round_records.rationale:
Long contexts overload DRAM with large KV caches and saturate bandwidth, yet uniformly truncating or naively compressing distant tokens harms the ability to retrieve far-away facts. PHKV cuts bandwidth by (a) pyramiding older tokens into a tiny number of multi-aspect summaries, so the attention kernel rarely touches large blocks of KVs, and (b) using a learned predictor to avoid moving data that will not affect the softmax—only when the model is likely to need specificity do we fetch a small set of exact KVs. This preserves recall for distant evidence because every chunk remains represented by semantically diverse keys, and exact tokens can be reinstated on demand within a strict bandwidth budget.

Robustness comes from aligning training and inference: the model is explicitly exposed to partial/summarized caches and learns to treat missing tokens as zero-padded while relying on summaries, eliminating the distribution shift that typically degrades performance under cache thinning. Pseudo-relevance supervision and similarity-based gating provide a practical signal for what to keep, while the ANN index ensures that even if a useful token was evicted, it remains recoverable with low overhead. Together, these mechanisms maintain long-range attention quality while substantially reducing memory movement.

inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: predictor-guided selective loading under bandwidth constraints
robustness to partial inputs via zero-padding and token-drop alignment
multi-aspect summary embeddings for a sequence via multiple viewer tokens
pyramid downsampling to progressively reduce resolution with age
pseudo-relevance labeling of historical context to guide selection
ANN index for on-the-fly retrieval of evicted context
cosine-similarity gated filtering of irrelevant history items

round_records.novelty_reasoning:
The proposed PHKV method combines: (1) a sliding window of exact recent KVs, (2) multi-aspect summary slots per chunk via clustering/low-rank factorization with hierarchical pyramid-merging, (3) a learned MLP predictor for eviction/recall decisions, (4) ANN-indexed off-DRAM exact KV retrieval on demand, and (5) KV-dropout fine-tuning with pseudo-relevance supervision. 

RetrievalAttention (2024) covers ANN-based KV retrieval from CPU memory. InfLLM (2024) covers sliding window + memory units for distant context with lookup. TRIM-KV covers learned retention gates. HeteroCache covers hierarchical storage with on-demand retrieval. However, no single paper combines all five components: the multi-aspect centroid summary slots with pyramid-merging, the learned predictor using Q_t dot-products with summary keys plus recency/entropy features, the ANN-backed on-demand exact recall as a fallback from summaries, AND the KV-dropout + pseudo-relevance training regime. The combination of hierarchical multi-view chunk summaries (with centroided rotary phases) + predictor-gated recall + ANN exact-KV fallback + training robustness via KV-dropout is not matched end-to-end by any surfaced paper. The closest overlap is InfLLM (memory units + lookup) and RetrievalAttention (ANN retrieval), but neither combines the multi-aspect summary compression with pyramid merging, the learned predictor, and the training regime. This matches the Score 4 anchor: combines mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this exact problem, introducing named design choices (pyramid-merge summaries, predictor-gated recall with ANN fallback, KV-dropout training) not present as a combination in the literature.

Prior works:
  - RetrievalAttention (2024): ANN index over KV vectors in CPU memory for on-demand retrieval during LLM inference — covers the ANN-based exact KV recall component
  - InfLLM (2024): sliding window + distant context memory units with efficient lookup for long-context LLM inference — covers sliding window + memory unit retrieval structure
  - TRIM-KV / Cache What Lasts (2025): learned retention gate predicting token importance for KV eviction — covers the learned predictor for eviction decisions
  - HeteroCache (2026): hierarchical storage with on-demand context retrieval for KV cache — covers hierarchical storage with retrieval
  - ChunkKV (2025): chunk-based semantic KV cache compression — covers chunk-level compression
  - Multi-View Document Representation Learning (2022): multiple viewer tokens producing k embeddings capturing different semantic aspects — covers the multi-aspect summary slot concept

InfLLM (2024): sliding window + distant context memory units with efficient lookup for long-context LLM inference — covers sliding window + memory unit retrieval structure

ChunkKV (2025): chunk-based semantic KV cache compression — covers chunk-level compression

round_records.method:
Build a dual-scale memory for long-context decoding: (1) a recent-window cache that stores full-precision keys/values (K,V) for the last W tokens per head, and (2) a long-range bank organized into fixed-length segments (e.g., 512–2k tokens) where each segment holds a compact summary per head plus a small set of high-importance "support" tokens. At inference, attention over distant context first consults each segment’s summary prototypes; only if a prototype scores highly does the model optionally touch its associated support tokens. This keeps most memory bandwidth on the recent window and a tiny set of routed long-range entries.

Attach a lightweight relevance selector that runs alongside decoding to assign keep scores to tokens and segments. The selector uses cheap features available at decode time (position, per-head key norms, a down-projected query·key score, cumulative attention so far, and topic/entity cues) to predict each token’s future utility. Periodically (e.g., at segment boundaries or every M tokens), perform an O(K) refinement pass over the candidate set to retain—using adjacent local swaps to bubble up the top-K tokens/segments—and evict the rest. Before eviction, roll evicted tokens into the segment summary using an uncertainty-gated exponential moving average: if the selector (or an auxiliary uncertainty head) is unsure the summary already captures a token, its contribution is up-weighted or the token is temporarily kept as a support exemplar.

Compress long-range memory per head with a prototype low-rank representation. For keys, maintain a small dictionary of head-specific prototype directions (e.g., 16–64 per segment) and store each rolled-up token as a soft assignment to prototypes (or a quantized residual). For values, learn a head-specific projection to a low-dimensional subspace and accumulate summary coefficients there. Each segment thus provides: prototype keys, low-rank value coefficients, and a handful of support tokens (original K,V) for hard cases. At query time, attention first scores prototype keys; their weights linearly combine low-rank values to yield an approximate context vector. If the max prototype score or a confidence metric exceeds a threshold, the model optionally re-attends to the few linked support tokens to refine the result, amortizing bandwidth to rare, high-gain lookups.

Allocate memory budgets and compression granularities per head, since some heads are dominantly local while others carry global signals. Learn these budgets with a sparsity-regularized allocation during distillation: train a student that uses the compressed/evicted memory against a teacher with a full cache, minimizing next-token loss and matching teacher attentions on sampled heads/layers, plus a reconstruction loss for prototype attention vs true attention over distant tokens. A mild group-sparsity penalty on per-head long-range usage encourages specialization and yields stable head-wise budgets. Deploy with infrequent maintenance steps (at segment boundaries) to bound CPU/GPU cache traffic; most tokens are written once then either summarized or dropped, and most long-range attention touches only prototypes, cutting both storage and memory bandwidth while preserving access to genuinely relevant distant content.
round_records.rationale:
The approach reduces bandwidth by (a) sharply limiting how many distant tokens remain in full precision, (b) routing most long-range attention through tiny per-segment prototype summaries, and (c) performing eviction and summary updates only at coarse intervals. At the same time, it preserves the ability to use faraway context by keeping a small, relevance-scored set of support tokens and enabling a two-step retrieval: cheap prototype attention followed by selective refinement when the summary indicates a strong match.

Key design choices make this practical: a learned lightweight selector avoids scanning all distant tokens at full cost; an O(K) adjacent-swap refinement pass maintains a high-quality top-K keep set without quadratic work; prototype-based, low-rank K/V summaries retain the principal long-range signals per head; uncertainty-gated EMA roll-ups prevent premature overcompression; and per-head budget specialization focuses capacity on the heads that actually exploit long-range cues. Distillation from a full-cache teacher aligns the compressed memory’s attentions and token predictions with the original model, yielding strong fidelity under tight memory budgets.

inspired_by.borrowed_aspect: lightweight selector for core evidence selection
global attribute style summary via self-attention

round_records.novelty_reasoning:
The proposed method combines: (1) a dual-scale memory with recent window + segmented long-range bank, (2) prototype/low-rank K/V summaries per segment with support tokens, (3) a two-step coarse-to-fine retrieval (prototype scoring then optional support token refinement), (4) a learned lightweight relevance selector with O(K) adjacent-swap refinement, (5) uncertainty-gated EMA roll-ups for evicted tokens, and (6) per-head budget allocation via distillation. InfLLM (2024) uses memory units for distant context with lookup, but without prototype/low-rank compression or uncertainty-gated EMA. "Forget, Then Recall" (GSA, 2026) introduces gist tokens as coarse summaries with selective unfolding to raw chunks — this is the closest match to the two-step prototype→support-token retrieval. KVSculpt uses distillation for KV compression. KV-Compress and BaKlaVa address per-head budget allocation. TRIM-KV uses learned retention gates with distillation. However, no single paper combines all these mechanisms: the uncertainty-gated EMA roll-up, the O(K) adjacent-swap refinement, the prototype low-rank representation per segment with support tokens, and per-head budget specialization via distillation together. The GSA paper is the closest to the two-step retrieval idea, but lacks the prototype low-rank compression, uncertainty-gated EMA, and per-head budget aspects. This combination is non-trivial and not surfaced as a joint application, matching the Score 4 anchor — combining mechanism families with no surfaced published joint application for this exact problem configuration, though individual components appear in separate papers.

Prior works:
  - InfLLM (2024): Memory units for distant context with efficient lookup mechanism for long-context LLM inference — overlaps with segmented long-range memory bank concept.
  - Forget Then Recall / GSA (2026): Gist tokens as coarse summaries with selective unfolding to raw chunks — overlaps with two-step prototype→support-token retrieval.
  - TRIM-KV (2025): Learned retention gate predicting token importance at creation time with distillation — overlaps with learned relevance selector and distillation training.
  - KVSculpt (2026): KV cache compression via distillation with adaptive per-head budget allocation — overlaps with distillation and per-head budget aspects.
  - KV-Compress (2024): Variable compression rates per attention head in KV cache — overlaps with per-head budget allocation.
  - BaKlaVa (2025): Per-KV-cache budget allocation based on head importance profiling — overlaps with per-head budget specialization.
  - SCOUT (2025): Segment compression with checkpoint tokens summarizing history plus local mixer — overlaps with segmented long-range memory with summaries.
prior_works: InfLLM (2024): Memory units for distant context with efficient lookup mechanism for long-context LLM inference — overlaps with segmented long-range memory bank concept.
Forget Then Recall / GSA (2026): Gist tokens as coarse summaries with selective unfolding to raw chunks — overlaps with two-step prototype→support-token retrieval.
TRIM-KV (2025): Learned retention gate predicting token importance at creation time with distillation — overlaps with learned relevance selector and distillation training.
KVSculpt (2026): KV cache compression via distillation with adaptive per-head budget allocation — overlaps with distillation and per-head budget aspects.
KV-Compress (2024): Variable compression rates per attention head in KV cache — overlaps with per-head budget allocation.
BaKlaVa (2025): Per-KV-cache budget allocation based on head importance profiling — overlaps with per-head budget specialization.
SCOUT (2025): Segment compression with checkpoint tokens summarizing history plus local mixer — overlaps with segmented long-range memory with summaries.

global attribute style summary via self-attention

novelty.reasoning: Method A's strongest novel element is the uncertainty-gated exponential moving average for rolling evicted tokens into segment summaries, combined with per-head budget allocation learned via sparsity-regularized distillation. These are interesting but individually recognizable components (EMA summarization, head-wise specialization, distillation). Method B's strongest novel element is the ANN-backed on-demand exact KV recall path: evicted tokens are quantized and stored off-DRAM with an ANN index, and a lightweight predictor decides at each decoding step whether to fetch exact KVs back transiently and splice them into the attention computation. This "evict-but-recall-on-demand via ANN" mechanism—where the cache is not truly discarded but becomes a queryable external store with bandwidth-budgeted retrieval—is a more distinctive and harder-to-find combination than Method A's prototype/EMA approach, which more closely resembles existing streaming attention and token merging literature. The pyramid-merging of summaries across aging chunks also adds a hierarchical compression angle not present in Method A.

validity.reasoning: The failure mode is that large KV caches dominate memory bandwidth, causing models to lose access to distant relevant tokens when entries are evicted. Both methods address this directly: Method A uses dual-scale memory with prototype-based compression, selective eviction with uncertainty-gated summarization, and per-head budget allocation, with a traceable pathway from bandwidth reduction (prototypes instead of full KVs) to preserving distant relevant tokens (support tokens + prototype scoring). Method B uses chunk-based summarization, a predictor-guided recall system, and an ANN index for on-demand exact KV retrieval, with a traceable pathway from eviction (chunks replaced by summary slots) to preserving distant relevant tokens (predictor + ANN recall of exact KVs when needed). Both methods engage the failure mode head-on. However, Method B's ANN-backed exact recall path more directly addresses the specific concern of "preserving the model's ability to attend to distant relevant tokens" — it can restore full-fidelity KVs on demand rather than relying solely on approximate summaries. Method A's support tokens serve a similar role but are more constrained (fixed small set per segment). The two methods are comparably direct in their engagement with the failure mode overall.

coherence.reasoning: Method A has a coherence tension in its recall mechanism: it proposes fetching exact evicted KVs from off-DRAM via an ANN index and "splicing" them transiently into the attention computation mid-decoding. This requires a well-defined mechanism for dynamically inserting tokens into the attention kernel at inference time, which is non-trivial and not fully specified. Additionally, the "centroided rotary phases" for position encodings of summary slots is hand-waved — RoPE is position-specific and averaging or centroiding phases is not a standard or clearly defined operation. The predictor training via loss-difference pseudo-labels during fine-tuning is also computationally expensive and the pipeline interaction is complex enough to raise implementation concerns. Method B is more internally consistent: its dual-scale memory (recent window + segmented long-range with prototypes and support tokens), relevance selector, per-head budget allocation, and distillation training all fit together without contradictory steps. The prototype low-rank representation, uncertainty-gated EMA rollup, and optional support token re-attention form a coherent cascade. The per-head budget learning via distillation is standard. Method B's main weakness is that the "adjacent local swaps" O(K) refinement is an unusual sorting heuristic that may not reliably find top-K, but this is a minor concern compared to Method A's more fundamental gaps around dynamic KV splicing and RoPE centroiding.
